text: The defendants now appealed to the High Court. Mitchell K.C. and Cussen, for the appellants. The question what are the rights of employés of the Victorian Railways missioners who were in the service of the Railway Dep at the time of the passing of the Victorian Railways Comm sioners Act 1883 (No. 767)? The rights now in question given by sec. 93 of the Railways Act 1890 [(sec. 72 of the every such employé "shall be entitled to compensation or reti allowance to be computed under the provisions of Act No and to have his rights privileges and immunities saved to him if" Act No. 767 "had not been passed." The Full Court } held that the effect of that provision is to give to the respond who was dismissed for no fault of his own, a right to comp tion to be calculated according to the arithmetical provis (1) V.L.B., 4725 27 A.L.T., 25. 3 OLR.) OF AUSTRALIA. see, 16 of Act No. 160, i.¢., that he is entitled to get one month's H.C. or A. ' salary for each year of service. After Act No. 160 was passed, the Railway Department was declared by the Governor in Council to be a temporary Department, and therefore that Act did not " apply to an officer in that Department (sec. 2). 'The intention of ~ Act No. 767 was to put railway employés in the same position as ~ other public servants and not in a better position.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: There was no intention to give new rights. The proper meaning of sec. 72 is that a railway employé was only to be entitled to compensation or retiring allowance if he fulfilled those conditions which a public servant under Act No. 160 would have to fulfil in order to be entitled to compensation or retiring allowance. The words "as if" Act No. 767 "had not been passed" govern the words " shall be entitled to compensation or retiring allowance " as well as the words "to have his rights and immunities saved to him." The section is merely to preserve such rights as existed before the Act was passed, and at that time a railway employé had no right to compensation. The words "to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160" have a wider meaning than mere arithmetical calculation ; they are intended to import the various conditions imposed by Act No. 160.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: That was so held by the Full Court in Mills v. The Queen (1) as to the same words in see. 99 of the Public Service Act 1883 (No. 773), which was passed on the same day as Act No. 767. If that meaning is given to the words, then the plaintiff must fail.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: This is an action against the Commissioners, and it was not intended to give them any control over compensation. The words of sec. 99 of Act No. 773 are "superannuation or retiring allowance compensation or gratuity," and those identical words are used in the Discipline Act 1883 (No. 777), which was also passed on the same day as Act No. 767 and Act No. 773. The words must be given the same meaning in each of the three Acts. "Gratuity" must mean gratuity as defined by Act No. 160, and "compensation" must be compensation as defined by that Act.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

Further, that decision is obiter on the 'question raised by the present case, because the plaintiff asserted "na right to retire at his own will, and the Court held he could not give himself a right to a retiring allowance by sending in his resignation. Act No. 767 itself contains all the conditions necessary to give effect to the provision that a railway employé shall be entitled to compensation.

text: The Commissioners may remove an Jemployé at their pleasure, they may discontinue an office, and »»they can fix an age of compulsory retirement. As to a retiring allowance, by Act No. 160 certain conditions are prescribed which _require the interposition of the Governor in Council. By Act No, 767 the Railways Commissioners are constituted a corporate body, and the Governor in Council can only interfere in specified -: instances.

In this case the Court is called upon to construe see. 72 of Act No. 767, which was passed on Ist November, 883, and is intituled "An Act to make better provision for the truction maintenance and management of the State Railways." 'That section is as follows :—" Every officer and employé holding office in the Railway Department at the time of the passing of + this Act shall be entitled to compensation or retiring allowance to be computed under the provisions of the Act No. 160 and have his rights privileges and immunities saved to him as if this Act (1) 14 V.L.RB., 940; 10 A.L.T., 148.

The concluding p graph of the section is :—* but shall for the purposes of this be deemed to have been appointed by the Commissioners with any other or further appointment." The reference there sec. 16 of the Act which provides that all persons appointed the Commissioners shall hold office during pleasure only. | result, therefore, of the passing of this Act was that the plan became entitled to the rights given by sec. 72, whatever but was liable to have his office determined at the pleasure ol Commissioners.

text: The first question which arises upon the ¢ struction of that section is primarily a grammatical one. whether the words "as if this Act had not been passed" 4) the whole of the preceding part of the section, or qualify only words " have his rights privileges and immunities saved to hil The words are open to either construction, but on reference to th Public Service Act 1883, passed on the same day, the difficulty OF AUSTRALIA. s classified or unclassified holding offices in any department e public service at the time of the passing of this Act," t certain persons mentioned, "shall be entitled to supertion or retiring allowance compensation or gratuity to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160,'—those are the very words of sec. 72 of Act No. 767—* but save as aforesaid nothing in this Act shall in any way affect alter or vary the firstmentioned Act so as to give any person appointed hereunder any claim to any pension superannuation or other allowance." 'Two "days later, on the 3rd November, 1883, the legislature passed "another Act, the Discipline Act 1883 (No. 777), by sec. 11 of "which it was provided that :—" All persons permanently engaged serve in the naval or military forces of Victoria at the time of passing of an Act intituled 'An Act to abolish the payment 'of Pensions and Superannuation or other Allowances in the case sof persons hereafter entering the Public Service' shall be entitled jtv superannuation or retiring allowance compensation or gratuity eto be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160." It is perfectly obvious to my mind that the legislature, in passing those »Acts dealing with different branches of the Public Service on or about the same day, intended by the same words to convey the esame ideas. I think it follows that the words in sec. 72 of Act » No. 767 "as if this Act had not been passed" refer to the member of the sentence to which they.are attached, viz. "have his tights privileges and immunities saved to him," and do not qualify previous words of the section. 'There is another reason for coming to that conclusion. If he first member of the sentence is qualified by the conciuding rds it has no operation at all, for the persons concerned had no right to compensation or retiring allowance, and to say ; they shall have such right as if the Act had not been passed vould mean nothing. It is true that it is very difficult to say t positive rights, privileges and immunities these officers had a the time of the passing of Act No. 767.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: So far as they have brought to our notice, they had none. But it was apparently thought desirable to preserve any that they might be able to tablish. It is, in my opinion, unnecessary to decide whether 1905. ae Vicrortan Ratways ComausSIONERS v Brown.

text: Those words do not, in my opin the first words of the section. ; What then is the meaning of the provision "every offic employé holding office in the Railway Department at the the passing of this Act shall be entitled to com retiring allowance to be computed under the provisions of No. 160?" Those words are in themselves incomplete ; not explain themselves. Reference must be made to Act both for the purpose of determining what is the "compensation," and in order to discover the provision which it is to be computed. Act No. 160 was passed in 18 divided the Public Service into classified and unclassified Sec. 16 provided that:—* When the services of any 0 are dispensed with in consequence of any change in any Di ment and not for any fault on the part of such officer, if h been employed at the time of the passing of this Act in any for which a salary has been provided by the Appropriatio 1861, or if at any future time he held in his own behalf as acting for any other person any office within the m this Act, every such officer shall as compensation receive year of service one month's salary according to the rate of sal paid to him during the year One thousand eight hundred y-one or at the time when his services shall be so di with as aforesaid, and a proportionate sum for any additi time less than a year." The term "compensation " is also u that Act in reference to another matter and subject to ano mode of calculation, but it is quite clear, and it is not, in disputed, that the reference in sec. 72 of Act No. 767 is to which I have just read.

Department, and not for any fault on the part of the plaintiff. services were dispensed with in consequence of a change in a H.

text: C. of A. 1905. * First, as to the meaning of "compensation" in sec. 72 of vicrortay "Act No. 767: It is clear that that word is used to signify an 'indemnity given to an officer who loses his appointment not "by reason of any fault of his own. It is, to apply the analogy "of compensation for injuries caused by the exercise of Statutory powers, compensation given for an act which causes damage, and would be unlawful but for its having been legalized by Statute, » That is the meaning of "compensation." To whom is it to be «given? It is given in terms to every employé holding office at ~ the time of the passing of Act No. 767, and it is to be computed . in a particular way.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: For the purpose of making the computa» tion you must have certain data, viz., those prescribed by sec. 16 sof Act No. 160. You have to ascertain how many years of service _ the officer has, his monthly salary at a certain time, and, multiplying one figure by the other, the computation is complete. _ But another condition is sought to be imported, viz., that com. pensation is only to be given to persons whose services have been _ dispensed with in consequence of a change in a Department. _ Primd facie 1 can see no reason for the introduction of that limitation on the grant. It is, perhaps, possible to construe the section in that way, but, applying the ordinary rules of interpre_ tation, let us see whether the question is solved by the application _ of those rules. _ Irefer again to two sections of the Acts contemporaneous with Act No. 767, viz., sec. 99 of Act No. 773, and sec. 11 of _ Act No. 777.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: I have already pointed out that the provisions of 'those two sections are in identical terms with the provisions of sec, 72 of Act 767, so far as compensation and retiring allowance "are concerned, and I think it is impossible to hold that the ~ legislature meant to give different rights under provisions passed at the same time and expressed in the same words. If therefore we can discover what those words mean in any one of those three sections, we have the key to what they mean in the others. Iturn first to see, 11 of Act No. 777. [His Honor read the section 'and continued].

text: By sec. 27 of Act No. was provided that:—"After the passing of this Act noo the civil service shall be dismissed therefrom, or suffer any penalty in respect thereof, except for the causes and in the mar set forth in this Act; but nothing herein contained shall be ta to prevent the Governor in Council, if it be expedient to re the number of officers in any department or to amalgamate | or more departments, from dispensing with the services of officers in consequence of any such alteration." Therefore, th was no power under that Act to dispense with the services 0 officer except in the two cases mentioned, and in those case officer whose services were dispensed with was entitled under 16 tocompensation. By Act 773 first of all Act No. 160 is rep but sec. 2 which repeals it, does so "save and except as to matters and things done under and to all the privil rights now existing or hereafter accruing of all persons" subject to the provisions of that Act." Therefore the rig compensation on having services dispensed with under the ¢ cumstances mentioned in sees. 16 and 27 of Act No. 160 preserved, and no other enactment was required to preserv right. Those sections applied to classified officers only. it is provided by sec. 99 that :—* All persons classi unclassified holding offices in any department of the publie at the time of the passing of this Act . . . shall be er to superannuation or retiring allowance compensation or g to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160." Ther of all persons (i.¢., classified officers) who had any under 160 were preserved by sec. 2 of Act No. 773, and some righ terms conferred, not only upon those persons, but also 1 unclassified officers who held office at the passing of the A How is it possible, applying the ordinary rules of interpreta on construe sec. 99 as meaning anything else than a confer new right?
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: Otherwise, the section would, in respect b OF AUSTRALIA. and unclassified persons, be entirely nugatory. 'The x had the right without that section and the latter had Turning now to sec. 72 of Act No. 767, we find that cisely the same right is conferred in the same language. It aright of every officer and employé to receive compensation retiring allowance to be computed under the provisions of Act 160. I think it is manifest, applying the ordinary rules of pretation, that the intention of the legislature is clearly ssed to confer a new right—to put the two branches of the service on the same footing, and to say that all officers shall be ntitled to compensation to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160, that is, to indemnity on being deprived of office.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: No. 773 allowed the services of officers to be dispensed with nder certain circumstances. Sec. 76 of that Act enlarged the ses in which the Governor in Council might dispense with the vices of officers. That power was no longer limited to the s of dispensing with services in consequence of the reducon of the number of officers in any Department or in consequence of the amalgamation of two or more Departments, as provided by e. 27 of Act No. 160. But by sec. 76 it was provided that -— After the passing of this Act no officer in the public service shall be dismissed therefrom or suffer any other penalty in respect thereof except for the causes and in the manner set forth in this Act; but nothing herein contained shall be taken to prevent the 'd with the consent of the Governor in Council reducing the mber of officers in any department or dispensing with the ervices of any officers or amalgamating two or more departments." at under Act No. 773, first of all, all rights of officers are erved by sec. 2, then power is given by sec. 76 to dispense the services of any officers, and then there is a general ovision in sec. 99 that all officers shall be entitled to compensation. It appears to me that the legislature uses the term 'compensation " as synonymous with "allowance in the event of ices being dispensed with otherwise' than by the officer's fault." It seems to me it is perfectly irrelevant to introduce ito the computation of compensation for services being disnsed with, the reason for those services being dispensed vith.

text: I think therefore that the plaintiff's services were liabl dispensed with, and that the services of other persons are h liable to be dispensed with, at the pleasure of the Commissi but only subject to the liability to make compensation. I therefore that the decision of the Supreme Court is right, Barton J. This is a case arising under the Victorian Rail Commissioners Act 1883 (No. 767), and raising a question whe an employé in the Railway Service who was in that Service time of the passing of that Act, 1st November, 1883, i under the provisions of sec. 72 of that Act to compensation u1 these circumstances :—The plaintiff was employed in the | way Department or Service—I shall not make any disti between those two terms—from 1866.

text: He had passed various grades of that Service at the time Act No. 767 was Up to that time there was no law under which the plaintiff e be entitled to any retiring allowance or compensation in the e of the Governor in Council terminating his employment, or of! retiring from the service. In July, 1903, the Commission formed the plaintiffin writing that they had, "under the provi: of the Railways Acts, determined his employment and rem him from their service," and this intimation is accepted on b sides as having deprived him of his office or employment. Itis asserted that he was dismissed for any misconduct.

text: No of any such kind or of breach of the regulations was made agail him, nor were his services dispensed with in consequence of change in the Department or of any reduction in the num! officers in the Department. For the-purposes of this ease, i be taken that the plaintiff was put out of his employment wit any fault of his own, and merely because the Commissioner the exercise of their judgment, thought fit to dispense with services. . Among the several Acts referred to in argument are two Service Acts to which reference is necessary at this point, Act No. 160 and Act No. 773.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: Act No. 160 was passed in for the purpose of classifying the Civil Service, regulati OF AUSTRALIA. appointment, promotion and dismissal, granting furlough to , and, the preamble adds, providing retiring allowances for sin certain cases, although the Act went beyond that in oviding for something more than retiring allowances. It was provided by sec. 1 that the Act should not apply to (among others) "any officer who is now or hereafter shall be in any ha Department which the Governor in Council shall declare temrary." Later in the same year the Railway Department was declared by the Governor in Council to be temporary, and therenorailway servant could have the advantage of Act No. 160. 'There was then no other Act by which a right to retiring allowance _ or compensation was given,so that railway servants were dependent pon the bounty of Parliament in that respect until the passing af Act No. 767 now in question. Sec. 16 of Act No. 160 will have to be considered in connection with sec. 72 of Act No. 767. 'See. 16 provides that:—[His Honor read the section and connued.] It will now be convenient to read sec. 72 of Act No. 767 use of its close connection in argument with sec. 16 of Act ~ No. 160.
citation: Victorian Railways Commissioners v Brown [1905] HCA 51

text: Sec. 72 provides that :—[His Honor read the section "and eontinued.] The question that arises is shortly this. The "plaintiff's services having been dispensed with in the manner I "have stated, is he entitled to compensation or retiring allowance "to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160? * It will be seen that in see. 16 of Act No. 160 there are certain mditions as well as a certain provision for compensation. No ubt, while that Act lasted, many persons claimed compensation under it, none of whom could be at that time railway servants, they had to show that their services were dispensed with inconuence of some change in a department and not for any fault of their own.

text: It is indeed more grammatical and infinitely more reasonable OF AUSTRALIA. he sentence to which they naturally apply, that is, the immedi'ately antecedent words, " and have his rights privileges and immunities saved to him." It may be conceded that there is no particular evidence to show what rights, privileges and jmmunities these railway servants had before Act No. 767 was » passed. 'There may have been some which had been gained under the practice or regulations of the Department, and which Parliament thought were worth saving. However that may be, I come to the conclusion upon the mere words of the section, first, that a grant of a new right was made to railway servants— aright to compensation or retiring allowance—and secondly, that: + that compensation is to be computed, that is to say, reckoned, »» according to Act No. 160. If that is so, since the plaintiff was » removed from the service for no fault of his own, and, we are informed, before having reached an age at which he might retire, he is entitled to compensation to be computed at the rate of one month's salary for each year of service, if we are satisfied of one thing, viz., that compensation means payment for loss of office. ___ As the provisions of sec. 16 of Act No. 160 have been imported _ into sec, 72 of Act No. 767 for the purpose of computation of _ compensation, in ascertaining the meaning of "compensation " in i _ sec. 72 we may look at the meaning it has in sec. 16.

text: There can be no question that, from the context, although " compensation" is used in sec. 16 by itself, it means compensation for loss of office, because it has to be computed at the rate of one month's salary for _ each year of service. Itisscarcely conceivable that compensation % be given to a person whose services haye been dispensed with _ at the rate of one month's salary for each year of service, is not ~ compensation for deprivation of his office. It is not necessary to . toil through the other sections of the Act to trace a definition of "compensation," because we have in that section a sufficiently : clear indication of what it is. 'Then it may be said that there is another matter to look to, that is, the right to retiring allowance, and that it must be shown that ty there are provisions under which the plaintiff, if he had reached a lawfully specified age of retiring, would have been justified in claiming retiring allowance.

text: HIGH COURT with reference to retiring allowance and superannuation, » sec, 44 which fixes the annual allowance to every super: officer, in these words :—" Every superannuated officer (exee hereinbefore expressly provided) whether his remuneratior computed by day pay weekly wages or annual salary shall in respect of such superannuation the following annual alloy (that is to say) :—After ten years' service and under eleven ten-sixtieths of the average annual salary received by himd three years preceding his superannuation ;" with an additi one-sixtieth for each additional ten years or less of service unti has served forty years," but the total amount of any super tion allowance shall in no case exceed forty-sixtieths of the sal on which the allowance is computed." I mention that mere show that there is provision for retiring allowance in Act No, It is true that the section uses the words " except as hereinbe expressly provided." Those words appear to refer to sec. 40, wh is limited as to the time of its effectiveness toa period of ten yei which expired in 1872. It provides that "any officer who a time of the passing of this Act has attained, or within ten y thereafter shall have attained the age of sixty years, if or as so as he shall have been ten years in the Civil Service of Victoria of the district of Port Phillip or of both, and if he has not any other compensation or retiring allowance in respect of service, shall retire from active service on an annual allowanei half of the average annual salary received by him during the years preceding his superannuation." We need not be troubl about that section any further as it ceased to govern new cas after 1872. There is now therefore a complete scale of allowances in sec. 44.

text: Are we then to conclude that te such as these mean the same thing in two Acts and a to different thing in a third, all three Acts having been p the legislature, so to say, uno flatu? But there is a distinction of another character to be found in ec. 70 of Act No. 773, which provides that :—* Any officer loyed in the Education Department or teacher in any State ol who at the time of the Act No. 710 coming into operation the respective positions of officer or teacher in such Departit shall be entitled to a retiring allowance under the conditions the Act No. 447 and to be computed under the provisions of Act No. 160." text: In ect of the provisions of sec. 11 of Act No. 777, sec. 99 of Act No. 773, and sec. 72 of Act No. 767, Parliament has annexed no such condition to its grant. That, therefore, affords a strong inference the grant in sec. 72 of Act No. 767 was made without any condition except that the compensation should be computed according tothe scale in the repealed Act No. 160, that portion only of which revived for the purpose.
